timingInterpretationPolicy.guidance: When timingInterpretation.selectedTiming.scopeClass is narrow-hot-path, deltaPercent remains a phase-specific diagnostic. Claimability evaluates timingInterpretation.headlineProcessWall.deltaPercent when that end-to-end metric is available.

workloads.name: buffer-upload 16MB
workloads.description: Single upload command aligned with Dawn BufferUploadPerf WriteBuffer 16MB.
workloads.comparabilityNotes: Matched single queue write upload shape on both sides.

timingNormalization.note: Strict Dawn-vs-Doe operation comparability uses direct timing (left/right divisors fixed to 1.0).

workloads.pathAsymmetryNote: Doe upload may resolve to host-visible CPU writes without an equivalent GPU copy, while Dawn performs staging+copy. This hardware-path-specific advantage is non-transferable and non-claimable until structural equivalence is restored.

selectedTiming.note: Selected timing measures the comparable operation scope. headlineProcessWall adds process startup and harness overhead for the timed command.

headlineProcessWall.note: Uses timed command process wall normalized by commandRepeat and timingNormalizationDivisor. This is the end-to-end ranking view for one comparable workload unit.

details.workloadPathAsymmetryNote: Doe upload may resolve to host-visible CPU writes without an equivalent GPU copy, while Dawn performs staging+copy. This hardware-path-specific advantage is non-transferable and non-claimable until structural equivalence is restored.

comparability.workloadPathAsymmetryNote: Doe upload may resolve to host-visible CPU writes without an equivalent GPU copy, while Dawn performs staging+copy. This hardware-path-specific advantage is non-transferable and non-claimable until structural equivalence is restored.
reasons: workload contract marks pathAsymmetry=true: left/right use hardware-specific execution paths that are not structurally equivalent (Doe upload may resolve to host-visible CPU writes without an equivalent GPU copy, while Dawn performs staging+copy. This hardware-path-specific advantage is non-transferable and non-claimable until structural equivalence is restored.)
workload is non-comparable; reliability claimability requires comparability

workload has pathAsymmetry: left/right use structurally different execution paths (Doe upload may resolve to host-visible CPU writes without an equivalent GPU copy, while Dawn performs staging+copy. This hardware-path-specific advantage is non-transferable and non-claimable until structural equivalence is restored.)

workloads.name: buffer-upload 1GB
workloads.description: Single upload command for large-payload directional profiling (1GB).
workloads.comparabilityNotes: Apples-to-apples upload contract via command-stream Dawn delegate lane; both sides execute the same 1GB WriteBuffer workload with strict normalization.

timingNormalization.note: Strict Dawn-vs-Doe operation comparability uses direct timing (left/right divisors fixed to 1.0).

workloads.pathAsymmetryNote: Doe upload may resolve to host-visible CPU writes without an equivalent GPU copy, while Dawn performs staging+copy. This hardware-path-specific advantage is non-transferable and non-claimable until structural equivalence is restored.

workloads.name: buffer-upload 1KB
workloads.description: Single upload command used to align with Dawn BufferUploadPerf WriteBuffer 1KB.
workloads.comparabilityNotes: Matched single queue write upload shape on both sides; Fawn uses deferred queue sync to avoid per-command wait inflation at tiny payload size while preserving per-upload timing normalization.

timingNormalization.note: Strict Dawn-vs-Doe operation comparability uses direct timing (left/right divisors fixed to 1.0).

workloads.pathAsymmetryNote: Doe upload may resolve to host-visible CPU writes without an equivalent GPU copy, while Dawn performs staging+copy. This hardware-path-specific advantage is non-transferable and non-claimable until structural equivalence is restored.
